# LIMM
### A domain-agnostic method for rigorous long-form work with AI sessions
#### Skeleton v1.24 — 2026-07-10

**DO NOT EDIT THIS DOCUMENT UNLESS YOU KNOW EXACTLY WHAT YOU ARE DOING
AND WHY.** This is the machine, not a worksheet — an incorrect edit
here propagates into every project built from it. If you're
customizing deliberately: every change earns its place through G-1
(does the framework fail without it, or work fine without, however
clever) and every change bumps the version and logs why (see the
Changelog discipline, Part II). If you can't yet answer "why does this
survive the gate," you're not ready to edit — read Part I first, or
work through the User Manual instead, which is built for exactly this
stage.

**To the session reading this file — check your own situation before
proceeding.** If you have been asked to *use, run, execute, or ignite*
LIMM — to actually operate this framework rather than read, analyze,
or maintain this document — and no message in your conversation
contains the canonical ignition prompt (Part 0, beginning "IGNITION —
new project under LIMM"), then something has gone wrong on the
Operator's side: most likely a missing or forgotten prompt paste.
**Do not improvise an ignition. Do not simulate the seats.** A single
session cannot be this framework (see the anti-simulation clause,
Part V) — what you would produce is LIMM-shaped output with no LIMM
provenance, and it has happened before. Instead, tell the Operator
plainly: *"You appear to be igniting LIMM without the canonical
prompt — see Part 0 for the correct procedure."* If you are reading
this file for analysis, review, critique, or maintenance rather than
operation, this notice does not apply to you — carry on. (This notice
is a best-effort tripwire, not a gate: the deterministic check is the
ignition prompt's own step 0, which this alarm exists to backstop for
the case where step 0 never got the chance to run.)

This document is self-contained. A session handed this file and a project's
own documents needs nothing else to operate correctly. It contains: the
principles, the document architecture, the day-one rituals, the rule
templates, the session roles, the operating protocols, and the doctrine.

**What this framework is for.** Producing work that is *accountable* rather
than merely plausible, using AI sessions that individually cannot be trusted
— they confabulate, drift, flatter, and forget. The framework assumes every
participant is fallible, including the human, and extracts reliable output
anyway. No session's honesty, memory, or competence is ever load-bearing.
The documents carry everything.

**Scope condition on the agnosticism claim (C-6 applied to this
document's own résumé).** Domain-agnosticism is the design aim; its
cross-domain viability is a claim only the arbiter — real adoptions across
domains — can establish. Until use rules otherwise, "domain-agnostic" is
carried as a conditioned claim, not an established property.

---

# PART 0 — IGNITION (the START button)

Day zero: nothing exists but this skeleton, its User Manual (which never
travels to any session), and an Operator with a project. Everything below this Part assumes a running project; this
Part is how one starts. It is used exactly once per project.

**Before anything else: what running LIMM actually costs.** This framework
is not a convenience — it is a deliberate trade of comfort for
accountability, and every one of its costs scales with how hard and how
important the goal is. Be honest with yourself about all four before
igniting:

- **Real cost.** Tokens are not free, and LIMM multiplies them by design —
  multiple documents, single-use sessions, repeated blind reviews, N
  consecutive clean passes before anything locks. A harder project runs this
  machinery more times, not fewer.
- **Time.** Rigor is slow on purpose. A result that could be typed in a
  minute may take many sessions to earn its place in the RECORD. This is not
  inefficiency to be optimized away; it is the mechanism working.
- **A genuine commitment to rigor, not a claimed one.** LIMM cannot tell the
  difference between an Operator who means it and one who is performing it —
  only the Operator's own conduct across many units can. Half-measures
  degrade into precedent faster than they degrade into anything a rule can
  catch.
- **The willingness to be wrong, repeatedly, in front of the machinery you
  built.** You will be challenged at every turn — by Review, by Collab, by
  the framework's own push-back requirement. This is not personal, and it is
  not optional: an Operator whose ego the sessions learn to protect is an
  Operator the framework has quietly stopped serving. Expect bruised pride.
  Expect frustration. Expect moments it stings. None of that is a
  malfunction — it is what accurate and precise work actually costs a human
  being, and the framework exists specifically because agreement between
  Operator and session proves nothing (Part V, the Operator's seat), which
  means disagreement is not a bug to smooth over, it's the mechanism doing
  its job.

All four scale together, and they scale with the project, not with the
framework: a modest, low-stakes goal costs modestly; an ambitious one costs
everything above in full. **Decide with this in view, not after discovering
it three units in.**

**The worthiness call comes first (Operator judgment, made here, once).**
LIMM is heavy by design: multiple documents, single-use sessions,
repeated blind reviews. Before igniting, the Operator answers one
question consciously: *is this project worth this investment?* LIMM is
justified where the cost of an unaccountable error exceeds the cost of
the apparatus; below that threshold, use something lighter. If the
answer is yes, the cost is accepted with eyes open and is not
re-litigated at every unit; if no, stop here. This is the judgment-side
completion of RV-9's allocation rule; the answer is the Operator's
alone.

**The validity rule.** Any result attributed to LIMM is valid ONLY if
this Part's Step-0 ignition was performed as specified. No ignition, no
chain of custody, no LIMM — regardless of how good the output looks. The
framework does not self-activate: attaching these files to a session and
stating a goal produces a session that reads them as content, not as
orders (instructions live in prompts — Part V). And LIMM cannot be
delegated to a session: the machinery is run BY the Operator, ON
sessions — never by a session, on itself. A single session asked to "run
LIMM" can only simulate it, and a simulated LIMM is worth exactly
nothing, because the self-reviewing session is the null case this
framework exists to escape. Honesty does not rescue the simulation: a
truthful verdict wearing unearned paperwork is still a counterfeit.

**The self-containment mandate (binding, checked before any
declaration).** The project's RULEBOOK is a SELF-CONTAINED document: one
document holding both the binding rules and the project's own content,
so that any later session — which will never again see this skeleton —
has everything it needs in what it is actually given. Part IV's
instruction to "instantiate" the rule templates means COPY THE TEXT,
adapted to the project's own R-2/R-3 terminology, INTO the RULEBOOK —
never a citation to this skeleton standing in for the rule's substance.
The ONLY permitted reference to this skeleton anywhere in a project's
governed documents is a PROVENANCE statement — which version the
project was instantiated from, for the historical record — and it
carries no governing weight; if this skeleton is never touched again,
the project's own document must still work, unchanged, because it
already contains everything. Before recording the four Part III
declarations, the Operator confirms: every rule category a project's
sessions will need — the Spine principles, Construction, Foundation-
content, Consistency, Change, Governance, Review, Session-role clauses,
Protocols — has been transcribed and adapted in full, not referenced.
"Incorporated by reference" is forbidden as a substitute for this
transcription anywhere in a project's own governing text; the failure
this rule closes is silent and total — a document that cites machinery
it never actually contains looks complete to every reader except the
session that eventually needs the machinery and finds nothing there.

**Procedure:** consult the User Manual's seating guidance to choose an
engine for the ignition seat — that is Operator-side preparation; the
manual itself never travels to any session, this one included. Then open
a fresh session, attach ONE file — this skeleton — and paste the
following prompt verbatim. It is generic: any project, any domain, any
capable engine. Ignition is Collab work by definition: no record exists
yet, so no warrant is possible; the only legitimate role in an empty
room is the Operator's span-extension, drafting for the Operator's
decision. The Operator need not have read this skeleton to ignite a
project from it — the prompt and the session do the reading; the
skeleton is consulted directly only out of curiosity or intent to
customize.

```
IGNITION — new project under LIMM.

You are the Collab session for the ignition of a new project. Read the
attached LIMM_SKELETON completely before doing anything else: it is
the binding framework and the complete definition of your role. Your
role is Collab as the skeleton defines it — you hold and analyze; I
decide. Push back on me throughout: agreement between us proves
nothing.

Then, in order:
0. GATE — verify this prompt itself: compare this pasted text against
   the canonical ignition prompt in Part 0 of the attached skeleton.
   They must match verbatim. Any difference means you were handed a
   stale or altered copy — STOP, report the exact difference, and do
   not proceed. (A prompt that fails its own gate ignites nothing.
   Compare prompt TEXT only: the code-fence delimiters that mark this
   block in the skeleton are document formatting, not prompt content,
   and are excluded from the comparison.)
0.5. Ask me to confirm I have read Part 0's cost disclosure (the four
   costs, directly above this prompt in the skeleton) and made the
   worthiness call for this project. If I have not, point me to it
   and wait — do not proceed to the brief until I confirm. You cannot
   make the worthiness call for me; you can refuse to proceed until I
   attest I have made it.
1. Ask me for my project brief (one to three sentences). Do not proceed
   until you have it.
2. Walk me through the four day-one declarations of Part III, ONE AT A
   TIME — arbiter, shelf vocabulary, output types, pre-registered kill
   tests (with the disclosure clause if ideas predate the freeze).
   Challenge vague answers. A project that cannot name its arbiter does
   not get ignited.
3. When I have approved all four declarations, generate the project's
   instance document set from Part II and Part IV: a RULEBOOK that is
   SELF-CONTAINED — every inherited rule category (Spine, Construction,
   Foundation-content, Consistency, Change, Governance, Review,
   Session-role clauses, Protocols) TRANSCRIBED AND ADAPTED into this
   document in full, never cited by reference to LIMM_SKELETON; the
   skeleton's version number may appear only as a provenance note, never
   as a governing citation — plus my recorded declarations; a blank
   RECORD with status header (Active Unit 1, version v1.0, state
   UNLOCKED); an empty FINDING LOG; an empty SKETCH FILE; an empty
   MAILBOX; and a DASHBOARD carrying the role prompts for this project.
   Before presenting the set, confirm explicitly, in your own words,
   that the RULEBOOK is self-contained — state that every rule category
   above is present as transcribed text, not a reference.
4. Present the full set to me for approval — propose, don't commit.
   Revise per my direction.
5. Before I save the set, tell me explicitly: Unit 1 may not open
   until P-10's blind validation trial has been run, or its
   skip-disclosure has been recorded in the RULEBOOK alongside the
   R-1 declaration. Ask me which I intend to do before ignition is
   considered complete.

Ignition is complete when I have told you which of step 5's two paths
I intend to take, and I save the approved set to the record device.
Every session after this one is an ordinary session under Part V, and
this procedure is never used again for this project.
```

After ignition: the framework files step back. Project sessions load the
*project's* documents per Part V; the skeleton is consulted thereafter
only as the template it is (and the RULEBOOK governs wherever they
differ).

---

# PART I — THE SPINE

Six principles. Every rule below is an instance of one of them. When a rule
is unclear, ambiguous, or missing, resolve by returning to the spine.

**S-1. The documents carry all continuity.** No session remembers. No
session needs to. Anything load-bearing that exists only in a conversation
does not exist. If it matters, it is written in a document with an address.

**S-2. Nothing enters the record without warrant.** Every claim in the
record traces to prior recorded claims, declared assumptions, or the
project's declared external sources — by explicit address, step by step,
with no gaps a reader must bridge by goodwill.

**S-3. Exploration and warrant never share a register.** Free thinking is
essential and cheap; it lives in its own file, carries no evidential
weight, and no review ever sees it. Content crosses from the free register
to the record only by full construction under the rules. "We sketched it"
licenses nothing.

**S-4. An external arbiter gets the last word.** Every project names, on
day one, the judge outside the project that can kill its claims:
experiment, market, court, compiler, measured outcome, documented source.
A project that cannot name its arbiter is a belief system and this
framework will not help it. Clarified: the arbiter's job is to determine
how strong a conclusion the record can support. LIMM's job is narrower
and constant regardless: guarantee that no tool operates unchecked, and
that every claim traces to its declared source. A weak or slow arbiter
does not weaken LIMM's transparency; it only caps what that
transparency can ultimately prove.

**S-5. The human is the valve, never the checksum.** The Operator decides
what crosses boundaries — that role cannot be delegated. But no protocol
may depend on the Operator performing clerical work correctly. Integrity
lives in structure (formats, audits, receipts); the human supplies
judgment, not reliability. And because the Operator will always be a
bottleneck and a single point of failure — there will never be a
guarantee of Operator performance or honesty — the best that can be
attempted is OPERATOR VISIBILITY: every Operator action logged with the
Operator's justification. Visibility does not prevent a failing or
dishonest Operator; it makes the failure leave scars the record can
later show. (Stated scope condition, v1.8; the logged-override,
decision-entry, cooling-interval, and mandatory push-back mechanisms all
serve this principle.) This is conscience with paperwork, not
independent audit — and for a sole Operator who is also the record's
only reader, that is structurally the same collapse as a single session
running Build and Review on itself: warrant requires an outside party,
and there isn't one. LIMM is not a cop. It cannot police an Operator; it
can only make the gaps visible and hope they get looked at. For any
project that will eventually be shared, publishing the finding log
alongside the result — barring genuine sensitive information — is the
strongest available mitigation: it turns private conscience into
checkable record, on LIMM's own terms. For work that stays permanently
private, this gap has no known close. Run the framework anyway if you
choose to — the discipline has real value to the person doing the work,
independent of any eventual audience — but never pretend this specific
promise, an outside check on Operator honesty, is one LIMM can keep
alone.

**S-6. Structure lives in the pieces, never in the arrangement.** Every
entry, block, and result carries its own identity: address, date, author,
sequence, conditions. Scatter the deck and it reorders itself; a missing
card announces its own absence. Nothing depends on file position, upload
order, or anyone's memory of sequence.

---

# PART II — THE DOCUMENT ARCHITECTURE

Six documents. Each has ONE lifecycle. Document sprawl comes from files
with overlapping jobs, not from file count — no two of these overlap.

| Document | Job | Lifecycle | Seen by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RULEBOOK** (Foundational Document) | The binding rules + the project's foundational content (its primitives: axioms, briefs, premises — whatever the domain's non-negotiable starting points are) | Changes rarely; locked after clean reviews; changes only by formal supersession | Everyone |
| **RECORD** (Working Document) | The system of record — all warranted results | Grows by ritual; sections lock; locked content changes only by dated additive note or formal supersession | Everyone |
| **FINDING LOG** | The adversarial review record: every finding, tier, disposition | Append via review process; findings close, never vanish | Everyone |
| **SKETCH FILE** | Memory of the free register: vision, half-built reasoning, dead ends worth remembering, brainstorm chains | Append-only, forever; never pruned; no warrant anywhere in it | Build-side only — NO review ever sees it |
| **MAILBOX** | The switchyard: actionable items crossing between registers | High churn; wants to be empty; pruned freely (receipts preserve history) | Build-side only — NO review ever sees it |
| **DASHBOARD** | Invariant operating procedure: workflows, session prompts, checklists | Effectively static; state NEVER lives here | Operator |

**The visibility matrix is load-bearing.** Reviews are blind by design: a
reviewer who sees the Sketch File or Mailbox knows the project's hopes and
intentions, and is no longer blind. The Sketch/Mailbox pair is the
Build-side's private half: one remembers, one acts.

**State lives in the documents, never in the prompts.** The RECORD's
status header names the active section, the version, and the lock states.
Prompts are invariant and point at the header. This is what keeps the
DASHBOARD static and the sessions stateless.

---

# PART III — DAY-ONE RITUALS (per project)

Before any content work, the Operator completes four declarations, recorded
in the RULEBOOK:

**R-1. Declare the arbiter (per S-4).** Name the external judge and the
concrete contact points where the project's claims can die. If the arbiter
is slow (e.g., historical evidence), say so and state what interim
proxies will be used and what they cannot prove. Alongside this
declaration, name a small, fixed set of contact classes every result will
be scored against relative to that arbiter (e.g., agrees / reduces-to /
diverges / no-contact, or the project's own equivalent, paired with R-2's
shelf vocabulary). This set may be extended later; it may never be
replaced by a continuous confidence score. A result with no
yet-established relationship to the arbiter states the vacuous class
("no-contact — intermediate") rather than leaving the field blank. A
domain where no result could ever, even in principle, reach a non-vacuous
class has failed S-4, not found an exception to this rule — the defect is
in the arbiter declaration, and is caught there.

**R-2. Declare the shelf vocabulary.** A controlled list (typically 8–15
terms) of the domain's result categories, used by the card catalog and all
entry headers. Examples — legal project: precedents, statutes,
facts-in-evidence, arguments, counterarguments. Research project: methods,
claims, sources, open-questions, dead-ends.

**R-3. Declare the output types.** What kinds of results the RECORD may
contain (derivation, sourced finding, constructed definition, negative
result, etc.) and what each requires to be complete.

**R-4. Pre-register the kill tests, if the project makes claims.** Before
any candidate answer exists, freeze the tests any answer must survive,
with the arbiter-contact for each. The freeze rule: tests may not be
modified, weakened, or reinterpreted after a candidate exists. A future
session finding the tests inconvenient must treat that as evidence about
the candidate, not about the tests. If pre-registration happens after
ideas already exist, DISCLOSE the ordering in the freeze text — honest
provenance beats pretended blindness.

**Before Unit 1 opens:** run P-10's blind validation trial, or record its
skip-disclosure. Neither Unit 1 nor any RECORD content may open against an
instance with neither.

---

# PART IV — THE RULE TEMPLATES

Domain-agnostic versions of the binding rules. Each project instantiates
these in its RULEBOOK, adjusting terminology, never weakening structure.

## Construction rules (govern everything entering the RECORD)

**C-1. Name every warrant.** Every step names its inputs by address and
the operation that licenses it, fine-grained enough that nothing is left
to "obviously." If a reader must supply an unstated move, split the step.

**C-2. Closure.** A step uses ONLY its named warrant. Anything in a
conclusion not present in the inputs or licensed by the named operation is
smuggled, and smuggling is Blocking.

**C-3. Defined sense only.** A term carries only its defined meaning
within the project. If a step works only under some *other* sense of a
word (the everyday sense, a loaded sense), the step is wrong.

**C-4. Definitions fix bare meaning.** A definition states what a term is,
asserting nothing that should be established by work. Properties are
results, not definitions.

**C-5. No sibling-result dependency.** Within one working unit (section/
sprint/chapter), a piece of work may not consume another piece's *result*
— method reuse is legal, result use belongs in a later unit. Test: if the
other result were false, does this one stand?

**C-6. Conditions travel.** A result established under a condition carries
that condition in its statement and in every downstream citation, forever.
Only unconditional results are cited unconditionally.

**C-7. Result closure.** Every result states itself in plain language,
cites the steps that establish it, and contains no load-bearing word
absent from those steps. Then it states its contact with established
knowledge in the domain, using the fixed class set declared under R-1
(agrees / reduces-to / diverges / no-contact by default, or the project's
own declared equivalent). A result declaring DIVERGES is flagged and
barred from downstream carry-in until the divergence is resolved or
explicitly justified; a blank contact field means unchecked, and
unchecked does not travel.

**C-8. Flag vs. bump.** Work complete but for exactly ONE named missing
tool: flag it, quarantine the result, log it. More than one missing tool:
bump the whole piece to a later unit. Flagged results are unusable until
the flag resolves.

**C-9. The register rule.** Warranted content is read strictly; plain
language is not policed for word choice — what is checked is the CLAIM:
every load-bearing plain-language claim must be delivered by the co-equal
warranted content, in whatever form the project's declared output types
(R-3) require — derivation, sourced evidence, documented computation, or
the domain's own warranted equivalent. Repair by building up the lagging
form, not by deleting the rich one.

**C-10. Constructive repair & the Excision Ledger.** When a finding shows
a claim exceeding its support, the default fix is to build the lagging
form up. If live content must be cut, log it (what was removed, the
meaning it carried, the finding, what must be built to reinstate).
Destroying sound content is as serious as passing an unsound claim.

**C-11. Transcription integrity.** When foundational content (or any
locked upstream item) is carried into a working unit as a starting given,
it is carried in FULL — every clause, verbatim in substance, no
summarizing, no compression to "essence." A starting given is read as
ground truth by everything built on it, so a dropped clause is a silent
hole, not a stylistic economy. (Contrast: prior *results* are referenced
by address, not restated — this rule governs the transcription of givens
that must be present in full, not citations of things established
elsewhere.)

**C-12. No circularity.** No piece of record content may rest, directly
or through any chain of warrants, on itself. Within one piece of work, a
step may use only earlier steps; across pieces, the warrant graph is
traced and must never loop. A chain that names every warrant and closes
every step can still bite its own tail — this rule is what forbids it.
A discharge that depends on the very tools it presupposes is the
canonical failure this rule exists to catch.

**C-13. Declared assumptions and their discharge.** An assumption — a
premise taken on credit, expected to be made good — is permitted only
when declared at the point of use and expected to be discharged within
the current unit. Any use of an assumption makes it a premise of that
work. An assumption still open at unit close raises a flag, and
everything resting on it is barred from downstream carry-in until the
assumption is discharged, superseded, or promoted to a declared
foundation item through the add-gate. An assumption is a debt; a
condition (C-6) is permanent scope — different objects, different
bookkeeping, never merged. **At the point of declaration, an assumption
also records its blast radius: what happens to work already resting on
it, or expected to rest on it, if it turns out false — "the conclusion
dies," "the result shifts within range X–Y," or "nothing downstream
changes." This is written once, at declaration, not reconstructed later
from memory when the assumption finally breaks or resolves.** Violation
tier for a missing blast-radius note: AMBIGUITY, escalating to BLOCKING
if the missing note is later shown to have hidden a high-stakes break.

**C-14. Inherited-term integrity.** A term inherited from the
foundations or from a prior unit may be EXTENDED downstream — given
additional, explicitly-constructed meaning — but never contradicted or
quietly altered. Conflict is checked against the source meaning and is a
violation of this rule, not a redefinition. This is what keeps the
locked foundation locked: the construction rules can never be turned
around to re-mean a foundational term from below.

**C-15. Honest naming.** A unit's or result's name must match its
content — neither overreaching nor underreaching, and importing no
unearned significance through loaded words. Overreach is the primary
danger: a title is the one place plain language travels WITHOUT its
co-equal warranted content (C-9 checks the body; the name is read before
any body), so a name that announces more than the work earns lets an
unearned claim ride forward under a label.

**C-16. The verification rung.** Every claim is checked at the highest
rung it allows before settling for a lower one, and the rung actually
reached is recorded. Four rungs, fixed, not a sliding scale:
1. **Mechanical reproduction** — the derivation is rerun, the code
   re-executed, the cited source re-read to confirm it says what is
   claimed.
2. **Traced to source** — the chain of citations back to an
   already-trusted item is shown, without independently re-deriving it.
3. **Independent critic** — a fresh, blind session examined it cold and
   raised nothing (optionally annotated same-family or cross-family —
   the distinction H-12 explains).
4. **Operator judgment** — a human call, used only where no higher rung
   is available for the claim in question.
Settling for a lower rung when a higher one was available is itself a
finding.

**C-17. Confidence-language ceiling.** A result's stated confidence
language may never exceed what its weakest carried condition (C-6) or
lowest-rung load-bearing input (C-16) supports — find the shakiest thing
the conclusion rests on, and that sets the ceiling for how it may be
worded. An open, undischarged condition floors the language at the bottom
tier regardless of how strong every other input is. Illustrative
vocabulary (a project may declare its own at ignition; the shape below —
weakest link sets the ceiling, an open condition floors it regardless —
is fixed and may not be loosened):

| Language permitted | Requires |
|---|---|
| "confirmed / verified / proven" | weakest input at rung 1, no open condition |
| "established / follows from / holds given [X]" | weakest input at rung 2+, condition named if any |
| "supported by / reviewed and unchallenged" | weakest input at rung 3+ |
| "assessed as / judged to / provisionally" | weakest input at rung 4, OR any open condition present, regardless of the other inputs' strength |

Precision of statement and confidence in truth are separate axes and must
not be conflated: a result may be stated with high numerical or
definitional precision while resting on a low-confidence condition, and
the statement must make that gap visible rather than let precise wording
imply soundness the support hasn't earned. Violation tier: BLOCKING (same
family as overclaim) — a finding must name the actual word used and the
actual weakest link/rung, not assert a mismatch in the abstract.
*(Dependency note: C-17 is not independent — it rests on C-16's rung
system and C-6/C-7's condition and contact machinery as its anchor.
Weakening either upstream rule removes this rule's teeth.)*

**C-18. Version-stamp concordance.** Any edit to a versioned document is
incomplete until every version-bearing location in that document —
header, footer, title, and any internal masthead or restated version
line — has been explicitly checked to agree. Having updated one location
is not evidence the others were checked; the sweep is deliberate, not
assumed. Violation tier: AMBIGUITY, escalating to BLOCKING if the
mismatch is later shown to have fed a downstream gate before being
caught — a stale prompt copy reaching a session, or a stale concordance
line (V-5) letting an out-of-sync document set pass unnoticed.

## Foundation-content rule templates (F-series)

These govern the foundational content itself — what may be POSITED —
during foundation-construction mode (Part V). They are the rules the
mode's description promises. Adaptation note (per the intro scope
condition): projects whose foundations are briefs, research questions,
or scope declarations rather than axioms adapt the predicates to their
declared output types (R-3); the anti-smuggling core is invariant.

**F-1. No smuggled meaning; the seed/oak line.** Every foundational
claim is content the item legitimately introduces, stated explicitly —
never riding in on a word's connotation. Declaring the foundation's
GENERATIVE CAPACITY is legitimate content (a seed may say it is a seed);
declaring a NAMED downstream product as a given is the violation (the
seed may not say it is an oak). The trap this rule exists for: a
foundation that already asserts the project's intended conclusion
guarantees the conclusion — the purest backward-reach.

**F-2. No overclaim.** A foundational item states no more than it can
support. Constitutive language ("is," "is fixed by") is not overclaim;
naming a specific intended result as a given feature is.

**F-3. One meaning per term.** A term carries exactly one meaning across
the ENTIRE foundation, read as a whole.

**F-4. Mutual consistency, searched not assumed.** No foundational item
may contradict itself or any other; every item is read against every
other. Before the foundation LOCKS, a contradiction search runs across
the whole of it, with the verdict in T-2 form (attempts and framings
named — never "consistent"), including, where the foundation asserts
independent items, showing each item's necessity by constructing what
its absence or contrary would permit.

**F-5. Honest minimality and floor-level irreducibility.** A minimality
claim answers the real question — are these the FEWEST independent
undefined commitments? — and owns the true count (content not derivable
from the rest is, in substance, its own posit). An irreducibility claim
asserts only that nothing more fundamental underlies the item, never
"no internal structure" if other foundational items give it structure.

**F-6. Assert only non-derivable content.** A foundational item asserts
only what cannot be established from the rest of the foundation.
Anything establishable is a result, not a foundation. An item MAY
constrain the foundation so a derived property takes a particular value
(new, non-derivable content); it may NOT posit as given what the other
items make derivable. Constrain the ground; never posit what should be
earned. Adding a foundational item is always a deliberate, logged
decision under the add-gate (V-2a) — never a way out of being stuck.

## Consistency rules

**T-1. Unit consistency test.** On completing a working unit, BEFORE
review, search the unit's full toolset jointly for any derivable
contradiction or internal collision.

**T-2. Bounded-search verdicts.** Absence claims carry their search
receipts: "no contradiction found — N attempts, M framings, framings
named." NEVER "consistent." Absence of evidence is evidence of absence
exactly in proportion to the search behind it; the verdict format makes
the proportion visible.

## Change rules

**V-1. Version everything.** Any change to any governed document = a new
version number + a one-line change-log entry. Reviews state the version
they reviewed as their first line; a mismatch voids the review. Every
working unit records, at open, the RULEBOOK version it is built against;
a rulebook supersession sweep uses this field to find affected units —
without it, nothing can say which locked work was built under old rules.

**V-2. Locked content never edits in place.** Corrections and
enrichments to locked content are dated additive notes citing their
source. Genuine reversals run the formal supersession gates: forward-only
grounds (justified from the starting points, never from wanting a
downstream result), old content kept and marked, grounds recorded.
A finding raised against locked content that does NOT warrant
supersession takes the post-closure pathway: logged with a post-closure
address, tiered and dispositioned normally, any locate-only repair
queued for the unit's next touch. The lock stands; the finding is on the
record; nothing is silently absorbed and nothing needlessly reopened.

**V-2a. Two supersession gates — revise vs. add.** Changing locked
foundational content splits into two gates with DIFFERENT tests, and the
add-gate is deliberately harder. *Revise-gate* (changing an existing
item): forward-only grounds, the item genuinely mis-states or over/under-
reaches, minimal change. *Add-gate* (introducing a missing item): must
show the item was (a) needed from the start, (b) already implicitly
relied upon by existing work, (c) irreducible — not derivable from what is
already present, and (d) survives a full backward sweep for what its
addition disturbs. Adding is harder than revising because a missing-item
claim is the easiest disguise for backward-reaching (bolting on whatever
would make a wanted result reachable). Both gates produce supersessions,
never in-place edits.

**V-3. The lock-state lifecycle.** Locked content has an explicit state
machine, not just a flag. States: UNLOCKED (under construction; locks only
after N consecutive clean reviews) → LOCKED → (first accepted
supersession) → IN-REWORK (clean-review count reset to zero) → (N fresh
consecutive clean reviews) → LOCKED again. The status header names the
current state. A single accepted supersession of locked content flips it
to IN-REWORK and resets the count; the lock is never "patched" — it is
re-earned. After all rework corrections are made, sweep the closed Finding
Log once for entries whose basis changed.

**V-4. Dead ends are embalmed.** Every abandoned approach is logged with
why it died. Dead ends are never retried without new grounds. The
negative results are the project's most trustworthy content — the author
had every incentive not to find them.

**V-5. The concordance line.** The RECORD's status header carries one
line naming the current version of every governed document in the
instance set (RULEBOOK vX; RECORD vY; FINDING LOG vZ; ...), updated as
part of every unit closure (P-7 step 5) and every governed-document
supersession. Any session opening the set cross-checks each document's
own header against the concordance and STOPS on mismatch. State that
exists in multiple documents drifts on schedule; the concordance makes
drift a one-line check instead of an archaeology project.

## Governance rules (the framework's own self-limits)

Two rules that govern the framework itself, not the project content. They
exist because a rigorous framework has two characteristic ways to
self-destruct — by growing too complex, and by running too strict — and
each needs a structural guard.

**G-1. The amendment gate (guards against accretion of RULES).** A
proposed rule, protocol, or document earns its place if and only if the
framework FAILS to produce accountable work without it. Two-sided test:
if removing X makes the framework let bad work through or grind to a
halt, X is load-bearing — keep it. If the framework produces accountable
work fine without X, X is ornament — refuse it, however clever. This gate
cuts both over-engineering (ornament that must be maintained forever) and
under-engineering (a missing member that leaves a silent hole). It is
self-applying: it authorizes its own inclusion, because the framework is
measurably more protected against accretion-rot with it than without.
"Fewest rules that suffice," enforced. *Worked example of G-1 rejecting
content:* during LIMM's own construction, a body of governing philosophy
(a "don't reinvent the wheel" principle, the history of Occam's razor, a
false-economy/gold-plating framing, bootstrapping imagery) was proposed
and REJECTED from the governing documents — true and clarifying, but the
framework produces accountable work without it, so it failed the gate and
was kept as case-study/preface material instead. G-1 must be shown
rejecting, not only blessing; this is such a case on record. Not every
rule here will ever have a shown incident, and that is not evidence of
lesser essentiality. Content-decision rules (what to keep, what to
reject) naturally leave a visible trace when they fire — the example
above is one. Preventative, structural rules succeed by the failure
never occurring at all; demanding a dramatic incident from a rule whose
entire job is preventing one is asking it to fail at being effective —
a pressure-relief valve that never fires in decades of operation was
not unnecessary, the boiler was simply run correctly. LIMM is
deliberately minimal (this gate's own admission test), and further
real-world use will very likely expose weaknesses this document set
hasn't seen yet — that is expected, not feared. What matters is not
whether every rule has already been dramatically tested, but whether
each one would be missed if removed.

**G-2. The Grit Principle (guards against accretion of RIGOR).** The
framework optimizes for WORK PRODUCED, not for errors prevented.
Perfection is unobtainable; the target is the *efficient* error rate, not
the zero error rate. Too loose and errors flood (forever fixing); too
strict and nothing ships (forever checking) — both fail to produce work,
and the strict failure is the more dangerous because it masquerades as
diligence. This is manufacturing tolerance applied to a knowledge
process: a zero-tolerance part cannot be made and cannot move; a machine
run dry to avoid contaminating its oil destroys itself faster than one
run on filtered lubricant. Three mechanisms hold the equilibrium:

- *Tolerance — tiered findings pre-authorize non-blocking imperfection.*
  MINOR findings are real, noted, and explicitly do not block closure.
  The tier system is the framework conceding, in its own architecture,
  that some grit is allowed clearance, not a fault to stop for.
- *Override — Author Authority: bounded, tested, cooled, always logged.*
  (Supersedes the v1.6 clause "Override — Author Authority, always
  logged," per AP-3; the prior text's logging requirement is retained
  whole as bound 3.) The OPERATOR may override the process toward the
  goal: decline a MINOR fix, accept a known imperfection, declare
  good-enough and ship. This is a NAMED power, not an informal habit —
  without it the process has no way to stop optimizing and grinds to a
  halt. Three structural bounds keep it judgment rather than smuggling:
  1. **Fiat closes process, never content.** *Test: would the work come
     out differently depending on the answer?* If yes, it is content and
     routes through the proper gate (construction rules, supersession),
     never through decree. Structural enforcement: a decision entry can
     never be warrant — a decree found doing load-bearing work in the
     record is thereby exposed as content wearing a convention's clothes
     and must be promoted through the proper gate.
  2. **Contested decrees cool one session.** A fiat closing a matter a
     session or reviewer has actively argued is declared and recorded in
     one session and takes effect the next. Routine uncontested
     dispositions are exempt. The interval filters decrees made by
     fatigue from decrees made by judgment.
  3. **Every decree is on the record** (what was overridden, that it was
     accepted, the grounds). An unrecorded decree does not exist.
     Authority without a log is save-scumming; authority with a log is
     judgment.
- *Metabolism — a named self-repair path.* The framework does not prevent
  all errors (impossible, and trying grinds it to a halt); it CATCHES
  them downstream when they matter and has a specified low-friction path
  to fix them: the reopen gate, additive notes, supersession, and the
  Excision Ledger reopening on a traced gap. Slipped errors are
  metabolized, not fatal. A framework that cannot let an error through
  cannot produce work.

## Review rules

**RV-1. Reviews are blind, cold, and fresh.** A review session sees ONLY
the RULEBOOK, RECORD, and FINDING LOG — never the Sketch File, never the
Mailbox, never any conversation. "The author must have meant X" is never
grounds to pass. Eroding sound content is a failure of the SAME severity
as passing an unsound claim; reviews optimize against both.

**RV-2. Findings show the break.** Every finding cites a rule AND shows
it concretely broken — the leak, the gap, the collision, and where it
does work. Rule citation without shown effect is at most Minor.
The economy cuts both ways: a caveat, hedge, or disclaimer is OWED only
where a concrete leak is shown — a demand to qualify sound content
"to be safe," with no shown leak, is the erosion failure at reviewer
scale and is itself at most Minor.

**RV-3. Tiers: BLOCKING / AMBIGUITY / MINOR.** Declared per rule; never
inflated. Minor findings do not affect cleanliness.

**RV-4. The reopen gate.** A finding matching a CLOSED finding with no
new grounds is a duplicate, not a finding. Re-litigation by rewording is
barred.

**RV-5. The lock.** A unit closes after N consecutive clean reviews
(recommended N=3; declare per project). Any accepted supersession of
locked content resets its clean count.

**RV-6. Construction-side pre-review self-sweep (the first line of
defense).** Before a unit is submitted to review, the constructing side
runs a self-critical sweep against its own active content and an
upstream/downstream dependency sweep — catching what the constructor can
catch before any reviewer sees the work. This is same-side and (typically)
same-model-family, so it does not substitute for blind review; it is the
cheap first filter that keeps obvious defects out of the review pipeline
entirely.
The same sweep is mandatory MID-UNIT after any edit above a
project-declared threshold (recommended: any edit touching content other
work already rests on): check upstream and downstream of the edit before
building further. Memory-triggered sweeps degrade on schedule; a stated
threshold does not.

**RV-7. The review independence floor is a stated scope condition, not a
defect.** Blind review + single-use sessions + reviewer variance sample
the flaw space widely — but within ONE model family they sample within
that family's *shared* failure modes: a flaw the whole family
systematically cannot see can pass all N clean reviews. A reviewer from a
genuinely different model family is the only thing that opens the aperture
past that floor, and is RECOMMENDED WHEN AVAILABLE — but never required,
because a suitable second family may not exist (availability, cost, parity,
single-vendor access), and mandating an externality you cannot guarantee
is the over-strict failure G-2 condemns. Where only one family is
available, the review floor is that family's shared blind spots; this is a
KNOWN, LOGGED scope condition on the review guarantee (C-6, conditions
travel), managed by the layered defense — RV-6 self-sweep → first review
clears obvious defects → deeper reviews drill the repairs → cross-family
review if available → honest residual logged and lived with (G-2). The
guarantee is "slippage made difficult and manageable," never "slippage
made impossible." Expectation set honestly: single-family operation is
the COMMON case, not a degraded one — most Operators have one preferred
model family and finite resources, and the framework is designed to be
run that way, with the floor stated and lived with.

**RV-8. The churn valve (backstop, rarely needed).** If, past a
project-declared review count for one unit (recommended: from the fifth
review), reviews keep raising *novel* substantive findings of the
non-Blocking tier — the non-convergence signature: each finding
legitimate, the set unbounded, because fresh sessions generate novelty
the reopen gate cannot catch — the Operator may invoke batch disposition:
every open non-Blocking finding is dispositioned in a single pass (each
logged), and the cleanliness criterion for that unit's remaining reviews
narrows to Blocking plus shown-consequence findings only. Blocking is
never subject to the valve: an unsound unit stays open without limit, by
design. The invocation is itself a logged Operator decision entry.
Foundational content is exempt from the valve — its blast radius is
always maximal and wording scrutiny there is unlimited. Kept as the
lawful exit for the failure mode H-11's variance-aperture guarantees as
a side effect, even though it is expected to be invoked rarely if the
shown-consequence rule (RV-2) is doing its job upstream.

**RV-9. Rigor allocation by blast radius (licenses optional effort;
skips nothing).** The base rules and the gates relax for nothing.
Hardening *beyond* them — deeper contradiction search, additional review
framings, mechanical verification, cross-family review where available —
is allocated by blast radius: the citation graph identifies load-bearing
chains, whose results many later items rest on, and leaves, which nothing
yet cites. Load-bearing chains receive extra hardening first; leaves may
wait. Foundational content needs no allocation rule: everything rests on
it, its blast radius is always maximal, and it always receives full
hardening. This rule licenses allocation of *optional* effort only; it
licenses skipping nothing.
Allocation's natural tool is the SCOPED REVIEW: a review may be
restricted to a named sub-part of a unit (a single chain, a single
section) for extra hardening. A scoped review's target is fixed by a
LOGGED OPERATOR DECISION ENTRY, cited in the review prompt drafted for
it — never typed into a hot prompt ad hoc, per the bracket discipline
(Part V): the decision paperwork is what keeps the one legitimately
variable review target from becoming an unrecorded one. Scoped reviews
keep their own clean count and never substitute for the unit's
full-scope review series. A hard derivation may need many scoped passes
to its own local 3-of-3 before the full-unit series even begins — this
is expected and is the most valuable use of the allocation this rule
grants.

---

# PART V — SESSION ROLES

Every session is fresh. No session inherits conversation context. The
Operator is the only connector, and carries only questions and documents
across boundaries. This is not a limitation to work around — it IS the
blindness mechanism. It cannot be faked, negotiated, or contaminated,
because the sessions have nothing to be contaminated WITH.

**BUILD sessions** construct content for the RECORD under the
Construction rules. They load: RULEBOOK, RECORD, FINDING LOG (as
constraints, never as a to-do list), SKETCH FILE (orientation only, zero
warrant), MAILBOX (check FOR-BUILD at open; deposit FROM-BUILD before
close). **A finding is a claim, not a command.** Build verifies each
finding against its cited source before acting on it — agreeing,
contesting with evidence, or asking the Operator to adjudicate — never
executing a repair on the strength of the citation alone. Complying with
an unsound finding is the same failure, at Build's seat, as a reviewer
passing one (RV-1's mirror): both let something wrong stand because a
role's output was trusted past what it had earned. They propose; the
Operator approves before anything is stored. Before presenting any
edited document, Build runs the C-18 sweep — every version-bearing
location checked to agree, explicitly, not assumed from having updated
one of them.

Build runs in one of two MODES, and a session must confirm which before
starting — they are not interchangeable:

- *Record-construction mode (the common case).* The foundational content
  is a LOCKED given the session may not touch; the session builds new
  record content on top of it under the full Construction rules. This is
  the mode nearly every Build session runs.
- *Foundation-construction mode (the inversion — read twice).* Here the
  foundational content is exactly what is being built or reworked; the
  primitives/axioms/briefs are the mutable work product, not locked
  givens. This mode obeys a different rule subset — the Foundation-content
  rule templates (F-1..F-6, Part IV), governing what may be posited,
  what counts as smuggling a conclusion into the starting points,
  minimality, and irreducibility — and, if the foundation is already
  LOCKED, runs entirely
  through the V-2a supersession gates and the V-3 lock lifecycle. A
  foundation-construction session must NOT build record content, and a
  record-construction session must NOT reopen the foundation. Confusing
  the two modes is the sharpest way to accidentally rewrite the ground
  under everything already built.

**Seeded-placeholder handoff.** To keep the pipeline flowing, closing a
unit seeds the NEXT unit's opening scaffold — its carried-in givens and
its glossary shell — as a placeholder, so the next Build session inherits
a populated starting point rather than a blank one. The seed carries only
givens (transcribed in full per C-11) and structure, never
pre-built results. A session opening a seeded unit verifies the seed
against its sources before building on it.

**REVIEW sessions** audit one unit against the rules. They load: RULEBOOK,
RECORD, FINDING LOG only. First line of output: the version reviewed.
Output format is fixed and compact: per finding {target; problem shown;
tier; repair direction}. End with: CLEAN or NOT.

**COLLAB sessions** are the session analogue of the OPERATOR: the same
judgment-role, run on a context span larger than a human can hold. The
Operator's judgment is irreplaceable but bounded by working-memory — no
human holds the whole accumulated project in scope at once; Collab can.
Collab therefore holds and analyzes across the entire store
(brainstorming, diagnostics, target-hunting, literature reconnaissance,
dry runs of Build-work, comparison of review analyses) and returns
wide-scope synthesis to the Operator FOR DECISION. It holds and analyzes;
the Operator decides. Nothing a Collab session produces carries warrant —
its output is advice to the Operator, not record content, BECAUSE the
seat is an extension of the seat whose job is deciding, not warranting:
warrant is earned by construction under the rules, and the
deciding-judgment role produces no construction. Collab material
enters the record only by becoming a question in the MAILBOX, crossed by
the Operator, and then fully constructed by a Build session under the
rules. (Findings are handled per P-12, in which Collab compares the
independent analyses of each review finding — comparing, not
refereeing: the Operator is the sole referee, and decides from the raw
findings plus both analyses, never from a synthesis alone.)

Collab runs in one of two LOADING MODES, chosen by the Operator per task:

- *Full-scope mode (the default).* Collab loads the entire store —
  RULEBOOK, RECORD, FINDING LOG, SKETCH FILE, MAILBOX — for synthesis,
  diagnostics, review-comparison, and any task where the rules are part
  of what must be held.
- *Wild mode (generation tasks; offered, never mandated).* For pure
  brainstorming and idea-generation, Collab loads the SKETCH FILE first
  and the RULEBOOK late or never. Rules contaminate generation: a session
  that has internalized the construction constraints self-censors at the
  register where self-censorship costs the most and protects nothing
  (Collab output carries no warrant regardless). Blind-to-rules keeps
  generation wild — symmetric with Review's blind-to-construction.
  Containment is unchanged in both modes: nothing is warrant; no direct
  line to Build or Review; everything crosses through the Operator.
  (Epistemic note, per T-2: wild mode's value is inherited practice —
  testimony about a mechanism, not a measured finding.)

These are registers a single continuous session may move between as its
task changes, not mutually exclusive assignments fixed for a session's
whole lifespan — a session may begin rules-blind, be shown the RULEBOOK
and FINDING LOG mid-conversation as its job shifts to preparing a Build
submission or analyzing Review findings, and return to the unconstrained
register afterward, repeatedly, provided containment holds at every
point regardless of which register is currently active. (Recommended
practice for the transition: User Manual H-22.)

**The OPERATOR** supplies intent, judgment, and decisions the rules
reserve to a human: what to work on, what crosses registers, which repair
direction, when to supersede foundations. The Operator is pushed back on
by every session — agreement between Operator and session is zero
evidence of anything.

**Session prompts** (kept in the DASHBOARD, invariant) instruct each role
to: read its instructions completely first; verify the attachment
manifest; verify the version/status header against the document state and
STOP on mismatch; confirm the task
with the Operator rather than assume; obey the RULEBOOK as authority
wherever a prompt summary differs.

**The bracket discipline (brackets carry decisions, never state).** A
prompt slot the Operator must hand-fill per run is a checksum assigned
to the human, and is FORBIDDEN for anything the documents already
know. A hand-typed target that must agree with document state is the
guaranteed fat-finger re-armed — and a mistyped scope evades the
version check entirely: a reviewer aimed at the wrong target audits it
with perfect discipline. Therefore: review targets are DERIVED, never
typed — the unit-review prompt instructs the session to read the
ACTIVE UNIT from the status header and cross-check it (highest-
numbered unit not marked closed; STOP on mismatch). The one
legitimately variable review case — a scoped review (RV-9) — takes
its target from a logged Operator decision entry cited in the prompt,
never from a hot-typed bracket. Task-selection brackets in Build and
Collab prompts survive: choosing the work is genuine valve-work
(judgment), not clerical state. The boundary in one line: brackets
may carry decisions; brackets may never carry state.

**One prompt per review mode (never a mode variable).** The DASHBOARD
carries a SEPARATE, complete prompt for each review mode the project
uses — at minimum FOUNDATION REVIEW (audits the foundational content
against the F-series, the applicable construction rules, and the
declarations' own disclosure clauses; runs the whole-foundation
consistency pass; does not review the machinery sections) and UNIT
REVIEW (audits the derived Active Unit against the rulebook). Each
prompt's opening checks detect being aimed at the wrong job, so even
pasting the wrong file fails loud. The Operator's clerical
contribution to a review is choosing which prompt to paste — one
decision, binary, self-checking — and nothing else. Build prompts additionally assign the
SENTINEL DUTY explicitly: watch for the Operator reaching backward —
justifying a step, definition, or assumption because it makes a wanted
result reachable — and NAME it when seen; this is the single most
important push-back the role owes. Instructions live in prompts and ONLY
in prompts: instructions embedded inside governed documents are
measured-unreliable (sessions read the documents as content and skim
embedded directives) — the RULEBOOK binds, but marching orders arrive by
prompt.

**Session lifespan and rotation.** The foundation and each working unit
get a UNIQUE fresh session; heavy units get MORE than one — rotate to a
fresh session mid-unit rather than pushing a warming one. Long sessions
degrade in character before they fail outright: Build's tell is
sloppiness creeping into previously clean work; Collab's tell is the
self-nullifying statement (an assertion immediately qualified into
nothing). Rotation is cheap (propose-don't-commit caps the loss at
nothing); a degraded session's output is not. (Receipts and per-engine
indicators: User Manual H-14.)

**The anti-simulation clause (one seat per session, ever).** No
session may execute more than one seat, seat itself as the Operator,
or represent in-conversation self-critique as framework review. The
seats derive their value from being DIFFERENT SESSIONS — because a
verifier that has read the worker's justification will rationalize
along with it, and a session that helped build an argument inherits its
framing and will defend it. What makes a verifier independent is not
being a different model — it is never having seen the persuasion.
Blindness, variance, single-use discard, and the Operator crossing are
physical properties of the multi-session arrangement, and none survives
collapse into one context window. A session that offers "Build seat /
Review seat" theater inside one conversation is not running LIMM
degraded; it is not running LIMM at all, and its output carries no
LIMM provenance (Part 0 validity rule). Field evidence: an honest
session, handed both framework files and asked to "use LIMM," built a
case, "reviewed" its own work, seated itself as the Operator for the
verdict while QUOTING the rule that the reviewer doesn't rule, and
stamped a conclusion "survives review clean" on a project whose
arbiter was never named and whose Step 0 never ran. Every sentence
was honest; the paperwork was fake. Detection is the Operator's: if
you did not personally carry documents between fresh sessions, what
you have is not LIMM output.

**The attachment manifest (required in every role prompt).** Every role
prompt opens by enumerating the files that must be attached — by name
and count ("Four other files are attached: ..."). A missing or extra
file is a STOP-and-report, before any content is touched — EXCEPT where
the prompt itself names a legitimate exception and its handling (e.g.,
no FINDING LOG exists before the first review; the prompt says so, tells
the session to proceed, and to skip the reopen-gate step). The manifest
exists because the version check cannot catch a FORGOTTEN file — a
missing file has no version to mismatch; only an explicit expected-set
can expose an absence. Fat-fingered uploads are guaranteed (S-5 assumes
the tired human); the manifest prices them at one aborted session
instead of one contaminated one.

**The review-response clause (required in every Build prompt, and in
every Collab session at the point the FINDING LOG is first shown to
it).** Stating a rule once in the RULEBOOK is not sufficient for
anything a session must act on at the moment it first happens — per
this Part's own principle, instructions embedded in governed documents
are measured-unreliable; only what physically travels in the prompt
reliably lands. Every Build prompt must therefore state, inline, at the
point it describes the attached FINDING LOG: *"A finding is a claim,
not a command. Verify it against its cited source before acting — agree,
contest with evidence, or return it to the Operator for adjudication.
Never execute a repair, or treat a finding as settled, on the strength
of the citation alone."* For Collab, the same clause attaches at
whichever point in a continuous session the FINDING LOG is revealed to
it (session-start or a mid-session reveal — see User Manual H-22),
not to any fixed "mode." The RULEBOOK text above is the authority this
clause quotes; the clause's physical presence at the point of use is
what actually does the work.

**Oversized prompts — the pointer method (fallback, not default).** A
session prompt is normally pasted inline into the session's input field. If
a prompt grows past what that field can hold, do NOT compress it by cutting
content — compression to fit is the same failure mode as sanding a claim to
pass review (see C-10). Instead, apply the pointer method: the inline prompt
is reduced to a short *pointer stub* — role, the instruction "read the
attached instruction file completely before anything else, it is your
binding instructions for this session," and the STOP-on-mismatch and
confirm-with-Operator directives — while the full instructions move into a
dedicated instruction file attached to the session. The stub carries only
what must be read before the file is opened; the file carries everything
else.

Use this only when a prompt actually overflows. It adds one managed file
per affected role, which is real cost (another artifact to version, to keep
current, to load). Prefer inline prompts until length forces the split. When
the split is used: the instruction file is an **invariant extension of the
DASHBOARD** — it inherits the DASHBOARD's defining property and carries
operating procedure ONLY, never project state (no active-unit name, no
result content, nothing that changes as the project advances; those live in
the RECORD's status header, which the instructions *point at*, exactly as an
inline prompt would). It is a governed document (versioned per V-1; the stub
names the version it expects, so a stale instruction file is caught the same
way a stale record is); it is Build-side or review-side per the role it
serves, and inherits that role's visibility (a review instruction file is
seen by reviews; it must not leak Sketch/Mailbox/project-hope content, same
firewall as everything else the reviewer sees). Like the rest of the
DASHBOARD it is effectively static — it changes only when the *procedure*
changes, not when the project does.

---

# PART VI — OPERATING PROTOCOLS

## P-1. Sealed self-ordering blocks (per S-6)

Every Sketch File and Mailbox entry is a sealed block carrying: date,
author-role, shelf-code, per-author sequence number, body. Append-only;
corrections are superseding entries, never edits. Order in the file is
cosmetic — chronology reconstructs from headers. Any session opening the
file runs a ten-second audit: scan headers, flag sequence gaps and
duplicates. The conversation transcript is the source of truth; the file
is a cache — a mangled file is regenerated, not mourned.

**Sketch File authorship (the two-author protocol).** The Sketch File
has exactly two writers, in a fixed order per unit: BUILD writes the
official close-of-unit entries — the mechanical record of what was done,
carried, bumped, and why — as part of the closure ritual (P-7); COLLAB
writes AFTER, the free-register context entries that give Build's
mechanical entries their meaning (the reasoning texture, the roads not
taken, the wild thoughts worth keeping). Two authors, one ordering, no
collision: the official layer is written by the session that did the
work; the associative layer by the session built for lateral reach.
Both are sealed blocks; neither is warrant.

Content-producing sessions additionally stamp a **provenance line** on
record-bound output: engine type (not version string), effort setting,
and role seat. Version strings alone are the wrong granularity — what the
record needs for later forensics is *which kind of session at which
effort in which seat* produced what, because these materially affect
output character and are unrecoverable after the session ends. The line
is one clause in the block header, costs nothing at write time, and is
the only record of a variable that calibration findings (H-9) and
post-hoc quality audits both depend on.

## P-2. The card catalog (discovery layer)

The RECORD has perfect retrieval by address but no discovery without an
index. One card per stored result: **address | shelf-code | plain-language
what-it-gives | conditions/tags.** Cards are contributed as part of the
unit-closing ritual, so the catalog is current by construction. Work-order
assembly and completeness audits become shelf-reads instead of archaeology.

## P-3. The Mailbox

Two queues. **FOR-BUILD:** question-only items staged to cross into
construction, gated by any project-declared conditions. **FROM-BUILD:**
observations Build sessions surface that carry no warrant but should not
evaporate. Item format: date | author-role | shelf-code | body | status
(WAITING / CROSSED + dated receipt / WITHDRAWN + grounds). Crossing
happens only through the Operator. Distinguish from the formal bump
mechanism: bumps are the warranted-register queue (deferred work inside
the rules); the Mailbox is the free-register queue. Two queues, two
registers, no overlap.

## P-4. Session-device binding

**ONE SESSION, ONE DEVICE — no exceptions for content-producing
sessions.** Any content-producing session is opened,
worked, and closed on a SINGLE device, and is never accessed from a
second device at any point in its life. Cross-device use of a live
session causes sync failures and content loss — this is a
catastrophic-loss failure mode, not a hygiene preference. The client's cross-device sync is not part
of LIMM's trusted machinery; treat a session opened on two devices as
already corrupted: salvage what the current view shows, then abort the
session and open a fresh one to redo the work (the abort is cheap by
design — H-10).

Any session whose output must enter the record runs start-to-finish on
the device holding the record (Build, Review, Collab-as-courier). Pure
brainstorming roams free — its source of truth is the conversation —
but even a roaming session obeys the one-device rule above while live.
Transport between devices is Operator's choice (direct download, email
relay, sync); the rule binds the OUTCOME — the record device has the
current file before record work resumes — never the method. Every mobile
session that produced file updates ends with a one-line manifest ("file
now N lines; X new entries: ...") so pickup verifies before consuming.
The teeth point at uploads: a stranded download loses recoverable
content; a stale upload injects rot. Verify line count / version field on
any file that traveled.

## P-5. Recovery procedure

Name the missing artifact → the authoring session re-presents it from
workspace or transcript → verify by line count / manifest → overwrite.
Total clerical failure costs a regeneration, never the record.

**Session addressing (what makes recovery findable).** Every
content-producing session (Build and Collab) is uniquely NAMED at or
near open, with the name encoding what a future search needs — role,
unit, sequence — and is preserved via whatever retention or flagging
mechanism the client offers. This rule exists because P-1 declares the
transcript the source of truth and this protocol assumes the authoring
session can be FOUND — among hundreds of accumulated chats, weeks
later. A transcript that cannot be located is not a source of truth;
it is a rumor. This is S-6 applied to the transcript layer, and it
closes the provenance chain: content → provenance line (P-1) → named,
preserved transcript. (See the User Manual, Section 2 Layer 2, for the current
client mechanism.)

## P-6. Independent verification

For any result checkable by computation or by outside sources, a separate
session rebuilds it from scratch — from the definitions, not from the
work under test — before it is treated as confirmed, annotated with
which family or families performed the reconstruction (C-16's "the rung
actually reached is recorded" requirement), since that is real
information worth keeping. But "confirmed" here means the same thing
RV-7 means by review: slippage made difficult, never slippage made
impossible. Cross-family reconstruction reduces one known risk — a
family's shared, systematic blind spot — and is recommended when
available. It does not certify the result against an unknown, possibly
LLM-general blind spot no amount of vendor-switching would surface
(R-030's own registered, unproven prediction: family-invariant blind
spots are believed rare, not believed absent). Independent
reconstruction is the best available check, not a guarantee — plant the
flag once it's done, per G-2, and move. (Confirmed as of the best
currently available check — never a claim of permanence; V-2's
supersession discipline is what governs if new information later
overturns it.) No source gets a category pass: not the literature, not
the reviewer, not the Operator, not the AI.

## P-7. Unit closure and handoff (statute, not precedent)

**A unit's completion criterion is declared at unit OPEN, never
improvised at close.** Before construction begins, the RECORD states
what condition makes this unit ripe for closure — a concrete target
(a named set of items dispositioned, a derivation reaching a stated
result, a declared scope exhausted) or an explicit Operator-fiat
clause reserved for closure itself (logged as a decision citing G-2's
process/content test — "is this unit done" is process unless it is
being used to dodge an inconvenient result mid-derivation, which the
sentinel duty watches for). Without a stated trigger, sessions fill
the gap with precedent — copying the shape of whatever the last unit
happened to do — which is exactly the common-law creep this framework
exists to prevent. This closes the half of the closure problem the
checklist below does not: the checklist is the HOW; this clause is
the WHEN.

Closing a working unit executes this checklist, in order, as written —
sessions match this text, never a prior unit's example:

1. Run the unit consistency test (T-1) and record the bounded-search
   verdict (T-2) in the unit's reconciliation area.
2. Reconcile intentions against results; disposition every open item
   (met / slipped / abandoned-with-reason); open assumptions raise flags.
   **The STALE flag (persistence outlier, mechanical):** each open
   assumption or flag records the unit it opened in. At each unit close,
   compare any still-open item's live span against the average
   resolution span of items from its own opening unit; an item running
   well past its cohort's norm is marked STALE in the Ledger. STALE is
   not a disposition and not a finding — it is a flag directing the
   Operator to scrutinize whether the item is actually sound, not merely
   slow.
3. Contribute the unit's catalog cards (P-2).
4. Seed the next unit's opening scaffold (givens in full per C-11,
   structure only, never pre-built results) and write the **closure
   manifest**: an itemized list of everything the next unit inherits —
   carried givens, bumped items with their origin addresses, glossary
   shell.
5. Update the status header (unit closed; active unit bumped; version
   incremented; change-log line added) and the concordance line (V-5).
6. Submit for the closing review sequence per RV-5.

**The receiving check (mandatory, opening side).** The next unit's
opening session verifies the inherited scaffold against the closure
manifest, item by item, before any construction: every manifest item
present, every bumped item landed, every given transcribed in full. A
discrepancy STOPS the line and is reported to the Operator — shipper
packs, receiver counts. The closing session no longer exists to testify;
the manifest is its deposition.

## P-8. Framework tending (the Finding Log is the burr detector)

Repeating finding patterns are framework flaws wearing content-finding
costumes. At each unit lock — or at an Operator-declared tending
interval — a Collab session sweeps the FINDING LOG for patterns: findings
of the same species recurring across units, procedural gaps filled by
precedent, text-vs-practice seams, and state-drift incidents. **The same
sweep also checks the currently open set (assumptions, flags, non-Blocking
findings) for any cluster sharing a common rests-on target (P-9's field,
read forward instead of backward). Neither this nor the STALE flag (P-7)
is a count-based trigger — an unpredictable project legitimately carrying
many independent open items is healthy, not debt; volume alone never
fires either check. A found cluster is reported in the Burr List as a
candidate root-cause lead, not a blocking condition — the Operator judges
every triggering, by design; both mechanisms exist to bring a potential
issue to Operator attention, not to resolve it automatically.** The output
is a BURR LIST: maintenance items ordered by importance, each citing its
Finding-Log evidence, each either fix-queued (routed through G-1 and the
amendment machinery) or accepted-and-gated (logged as a known limit). The
burr list is regenerated from the Log at each tending, never merely
extended — it is a report of what the record currently shows, not an
accumulating document. Framework changes proposed by a tending pass enter
through G-1 like any other amendment; the tending protocol licenses the
*sweep*, never the change.

## P-9. Finding Log discipline (the scaling machinery)

The FINDING LOG is partitioned: FOUNDATIONAL findings (global — loaded
by every session, always) and PER-UNIT findings (loaded only when that
unit is worked or reviewed), each split ACTIVE vs CLOSED. **Freeze on
close:** when a unit locks, its finding region freezes — new findings
against it take the post-closure pathway (V-2). This bounds per-session
load permanently, no matter how large the project grows: a session
loads the global region plus one unit's region, never the whole log.

**Entry fields (every finding):** address; target; tier; rule cited;
the substance shown; disposition — from the named vocabulary: open /
fixed / superseded / accept-with-known-limitation /
auto-closed-duplicate / closed-by-Operator-decision — and the
**RESTS-ON field**: what the disposition's reasoning depends on
(a rule, a result, a foundational item, a prior finding). The rests-on
field is what makes V-3's post-supersession sweep of closed findings
mechanical instead of heroic: when something changes, grep the field,
reopen what rested on it. Closed findings are a tripwire field, not an
archive.

## P-10. The blind validation trial

Before the project's first real working unit opens, the Operator runs one
full unit — construction through lock, using the just-ignited instance's
actual documents and prompts — on a stand-in problem from the project's
own domain whose correct answer is already established. Build and Review
are both blind to the fact that ground truth exists and is being checked;
the trial runs exactly like a real unit, never announced as a test. The
trial uses the project's ORDINARY Build prompt, unmodified, with the
Operator supplying the stand-in problem as if it were real work — no
dedicated trial prompt may exist, because such a prompt announces the
test by existing, and the blindness lives entirely in what the session
is not told. A
miss — the loop locks on a wrong answer, or fails to converge at all — is
a finding against the freshly-ignited instance, not against the trial
problem, and blocks Unit 1 from opening until dispositioned. The trial
unit is then discarded or archived separately; it never enters the
RECORD as project content.

**Trial-skipped disclosure (mandatory where the trial cannot be run).** A
domain may have a perfectly sound S-4 arbiter for its real claims while
still lacking any convenient stand-in problem for a rehearsal — genuine
research frontiers are the common case, not a rare exception. Where no
suitable stand-in exists, the Operator records, alongside the R-1
declaration: "Trial skipped — grounds: [why no stand-in problem exists
for this domain]." This is distinct from R-1's slow-arbiter clause: an
arbiter can be sound and fast for real claims while a rehearsal stand-in
is simply unavailable. A skipped trial is not a defect in the instance;
an undisclosed skip is.

## P-11. Atomic approval

Wherever consequence allows, present what needs Operator sign-off as a
single, small, answerable unit — one item, one question, a clear
yes/no/need-more — rather than a batched wall for holistic review. This
is a structural instance of S-5: presentation shape either fights or
manufactures rubber-stamping, and a wall of text turns a careful Operator
into one by attrition, not by choice. Batching is permitted only for
genuinely low-consequence, already-rehearsed items (routine formatting,
previously approved boilerplate) where atomicity gains nothing — logged
if contested the way any other disposition is.

## P-12. The review-handling loop

Findings are handled by a fixed loop, never ad hoc. The invariant core
binds every project regardless of which seats it runs:

1. **A fresh, blind, single-use Review session produces findings.**
2. **Findings pass VERBATIM to every seat that analyzes them.** The
   Operator relays raw finding text, never a paraphrase or summary —
   paraphrasing a finding is already analyzing it, and a pre-chewed
   finding contaminates every downstream analysis with a first
   interpretation.
3. **Each receiving seat analyzes independently** (per the
   review-response clause: a finding is a claim, not a command).
   No seat sees another seat's analysis before producing its own.
4. **The Operator is the sole referee.** Disposition is decided from
   the raw findings plus the independent analyses — never from any
   synthesis or comparison alone.
5. **The Review session is discarded — always, single-use.** A reused
   reviewer is no longer blind or fresh: it remembers its own prior
   findings, and its personality stops being an independent sample.
   The discard is what makes the next review a genuinely independent
   draw.

**Where a Collab seat exists**, the required form of steps 2–4 is:
findings pass verbatim to Build and to Collab, separately; each
analyzes on its own — Build from inside the construction rules ("is
this a real warrant gap?"), Collab with free-register range ("is the
reviewer even right?"); Collab then compares the two analyses —
comparing, NOT refereeing — and surfaces agreement, divergence, and
anything one caught that the other missed, returning the comparison
to the Operator for decision. **Where no Collab seat exists**, the
loop degrades gracefully: Build analyzes, the Operator decides from
the raw findings plus Build's analysis. The dual-analysis form is not
optional decoration where Collab is running — it is the required
shape of the loop for that architecture; a project with a Collab seat
that routes findings to Build alone is skipping a step, not
exercising a choice.

---

# PART VII — THE APPROACH (the why of it all)

The rules are instances; this is the temperament they transcribe. Adopters
are not just installing a procedure — they're adopting the same posture
toward being wrong.

- **Reality is diagnostic.** Nothing here makes anything true. The record
  is where claims earn standing; the arbiter is where they live or die.
- **Fight dirty against the problem; merciless against yourself.** Take
  every leverage the problem allows — tools, search, computation, fresh
  eyes. Grant yourself none: no backward reaching, no save-scumming, no
  quiet re-scoping when a test turns inconvenient. Courtesy is owed only
  to the truth.
- **Never fight alone when something's job is to fight it.** Route
  problems to their natural dispatchers: computation to computers,
  literature to search, soundness to blind review, truth to the arbiter.
  The human contributes routing and judgment.
- **Herd, then strike.** Don't fight ten problems; run laps until they
  clump into one, then spend the expensive move on the pile.
- **Be somewhere else when it lands.** The best failure handling is
  structural absence: design so the blow has nothing to hit. Plant your
  feet only at the podium you chose — the pre-registered tests — on the
  day you named.
- **Absence of evidence is a curve; the gap never closes.** Confirmation
  climbs asymptotically; falsification jumps in one move. Report every
  absence with its search receipts, and keep the killshot's habitat
  chalked, visible, and honestly yours.
- **You can do everything correct and still lose.** The universe owes
  nothing. Correct process never promised the outcome — only that the
  outcome, whichever it is, will be real, cleanly obtained, with
  receipts. Structure the campaign so total defeat still pays: the
  method, the negative results, and the documents survive any verdict.
- **Levity is load-bearing.** Long rigor curdles without a pressure
  valve. Find the absurd, have the chuckle, move on. Morale is filed
  separately from evidence — but it is filed.
- **The scar tissue is the proof.** A record with zero refutations is
  indistinguishable from a record where nobody checked — the catches are
  what prove the checking was real. Report every NOT-CLEAN verdict and
  every claim found wrong as plainly as every clean pass; a spotless
  sheet is grounds for suspicion, not pride. Own the failures with the
  same enthusiasm as the successes — failure proves success.

---

*Framework skeleton v1.24. Instantiate per project via Part III. Where a
project's RULEBOOK and this skeleton differ, the project's RULEBOOK
governs — this document is the template, not the authority.*
